planting.note: Surface-sow seeds in early spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; do not cover seeds as they need light to germinate — Roman chamomile is a low, mat-forming perennial ideal for path edging, lawn substitutes (especially the non-flowering 'Treneague' cultivar), and herb garden borders.

directions: Chamaemelum nobile is a low-growing, apple-scented perennial chamomile native to western Europe, producing fine, feathery, bright-green foliage and small white daisy flowers with yellow centers from early summer through early fall; the crushed foliage releases a distinctive sweet, fruity, apple-like scent that distinguishes it from the taller German chamomile (Matricaria chamomilla). Surface-sow seeds in early spring directly in well-drained, poor to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun or light partial shade; seeds require light to germinate and should not be covered — press them lightly onto the soil surface and keep evenly moist until germination occurs in 7–14 days. The non-flowering cultivar 'Treneague' is propagated vegetatively and used as a fragrant, low-maintenance lawn substitute that releases its apple scent when walked upon; it requires no mowing but does best in well-drained, sunny sites. Harvest flowers by hand when fully open for use in herbal tea; flowers and foliage have traditional uses for soothing teas, topical rinses, and as a mild sleep aid.

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds in early spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; dyer's chamomile thrives in poor, dry, sunny conditions and performs best in lean soil — rich, moist soil produces lush growth but fewer flowers and shorter plant life.

directions: Anthemis tinctoria is an upright, bushy perennial or biennial native to Europe and western Asia, producing deeply divided, fernlike, aromatic foliage and an exceptionally long succession of bright, golden-yellow, 1.5-inch daisy flowers from early summer through early fall; it is one of the most prolific and long-blooming of all yellow-flowered perennial herbs. Direct-sow seeds in early spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in a warm, sunny position; it performs best in poor, gritty, or stony soil and will decline in rich, moist conditions — lean soil extends plant longevity and promotes maximum flowering. The whole plant, especially the flowers, yields a clear, warm, golden-yellow dye when used with an alum mordant; it has been used as a natural textile dye throughout Europe and the Middle East for centuries and was a primary source of yellow for wool dyeing. Deadhead or shear plants after the first main flush of bloom to stimulate continued flowering; short-lived plants are easily renewed from self-sown seedlings.

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds in early spring as soon as the soil can be worked, in deeply dug, loose, well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; burdock produces a very long taproot and requires deeply prepared, stone-free soil for straight, smooth roots suitable for culinary use — raised beds or deeply tilled rows at least 18 inches deep are recommended.

directions: Arctium lappa (great burdock, gobo) is a large, vigorous biennial native to temperate Asia and Europe, prized in Japanese, Korean, and Chinese cuisine for its long, slender, sweet, earthy taproot (gobo) which can reach 3 feet or more in depth; roots are best harvested in the first year of growth (before the plant flowers and sets seed in the second year) when they are most tender and flavorful. Direct-sow seeds 0.5 inch deep in early spring in deeply dug, loose, stone-free soil enriched with compost; the soil must be worked to at least 18 inches depth to allow the taproot to develop without forking — closely spaced rows of 6–8 inches produce slender, straight roots ideal for the table. In traditional Western herbalism, the root is used as a blood-purifying and liver-supportive tonic, and both the root and seed have a long history of use in treating skin conditions; the immature flower stalks can also be peeled and eaten like celery in spring of the second year. Do not allow plants to set seed and self-sow freely as burdock can become weedy in disturbed soils; harvest all roots before the plant flowers in the second year.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring in moist to consistently wet, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; meadowsweet is a plant of riverbanks, wet meadows, and marshy ground — it requires permanently moist or wet conditions and is an excellent choice for rain gardens, bog gardens, and streamside plantings.

directions: Filipendula ulmaria is a tall, elegant perennial native to wet meadows, riverbanks, and marshy ground throughout Europe and temperate Asia, producing pinnate, dark-green leaves that are white-downy beneath and spectacular, frothy, cream-white, almond-honey-scented flower plumes from mid to late summer; it thrives only in permanently moist or wet soil and is not suitable for dry or well-drained conditions. Plant rhizome divisions in early spring in rich, consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — it will grow in standing water at the margins of ponds and streams and is one of the finest plants for naturalizing in wet, wild garden areas. Meadowsweet has deep historical and pharmacological significance: the flower buds contain salicylates (including salicylic acid and methyl salicylate), the compounds that served as the chemical model for the synthesis of aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) in 1897 — the name 'aspirin' is derived from 'Spirin,' from the plant's former Latin name Spiraea ulmaria. Traditionally the flowers were strewn on floors to perfume rooms, used to flavor mead and wines, and taken as a remedy for fever, pain, and digestive complaints; harvest flowers in full bloom for herbal preparations.

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds in fall for natural cold stratification over winter, or stratify seeds in moist sand in the refrigerator for 4–6 weeks before spring sowing; agrimony establishes best when seeds receive a cold stratification period to break dormancy — it is a classic hedgerow and woodland-edge herb of dry, calcareous, or neutral soils.

directions: Agrimonia eupatoria is a slender, upright, rosette-forming perennial herb native to dry grasslands, hedgerows, and woodland edges throughout Europe and western Asia, producing pinnate, strongly aromatic, slightly rough-hairy leaves and long, slender spikes of small, five-petaled, bright-yellow flowers from early to mid summer, followed by small, hooked bur-fruits that cling to clothing and animal fur for seed dispersal. Direct-sow seeds in fall in well-drained, moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline or calcareous soil in a sunny to partly shaded position; seeds require cold stratification for reliable germination and will germinate in spring after a natural winter cold period. In traditional Western herbalism, the whole above-ground plant is harvested in flower and used as a gentle astringent, anti-inflammatory, and tonic herb with a long history of use for wound healing, sore throats, digestive complaints, and urinary tract support — it was a primary wound herb of medieval European medicine. Plants are drought-tolerant once established and perform best in lean, well-drained soil; avoid rich, wet conditions. The dried herb makes a pleasant, mildly apricot-scented tea.
sources: University of Maryland Medical Center Complementary Medicine Guide

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds in fall for cold stratification over winter, or sow in spring in well-drained, lean, neutral to alkaline, calcareous soil in full sun to light partial shade; lady's bedstraw is a plant of dry grasslands, hedgerows, and coastal dunes — it spreads by slender rhizomes to form loose, scrambling mats and naturalizes readily in dry, sunny, wild garden settings.

directions: Galium verum is a slender, scrambling, rhizomatous perennial herb of dry grasslands and hedgerows throughout Europe and temperate Asia, producing whorls of very fine, needle-like, dark-green leaves along square stems and masses of tiny, four-petaled, honey-scented, bright-yellow flowers in dense, frothy panicles from early to mid summer; the whole plant when dried emits a pleasant, sweet, coumarin-like (new-mown hay) fragrance. Direct-sow seeds in fall in well-drained, lean, neutral to slightly alkaline, calcareous soil in a sunny or lightly shaded position; seeds benefit from cold stratification and germinate most reliably after a natural winter cold period — established plants spread by shallow rhizomes to form loose, weed-suppressing mats. Lady's bedstraw has a rich history of traditional use: the flowering tops were used to curdle milk in cheese-making (especially for Cheshire and Gloucester cheeses, as a vegetable rennet substitute), and the roots yield a warm red to orange dye with an alum mordant, while the stems and flowers produce yellow; the dried plant was historically stuffed into mattresses and strewn underfoot for its pleasant scent and supposed flea-repelling properties. Tolerant of drought and coastal conditions once established; excellent for naturalizing in wild or meadow gardens with other dry-grassland species.
